text: article in that journal, to give just one example, was entitled, Is there progress in civilization?. His answer was, as always, a thoughtful and reflective one, anticipating later critiques of evolutionism, ethnocentrism and scientific positivism by decades. A few shorter articles and commentaries also appeared in Man (the first in 1907). He finished Rites of Passage by the end of 1908, seeing it through to publication in 1909. Rites of Passage: The Book and What it Says Rites of Passage is a relatively short book. It is also an elegant book, analytically sophisticated yet clear-cut, and constantly exemplified with references to a rich variety of ethnographic case studies described with brief but engaged detail. Van Gennep built his argument on the available ethnographic data, comparing ritual forms at all ‘levels of development’, without reducing them all to one universal function. Van Gennep here, as elsewhere, showed an intimacy with the theoretical and empirical literature on religion and society in the major European languages, including French, German, English, Russian, Italian, Polish and Flemish. Van Gennep starts out by suggesting a meaningful classification of all existing rites. He distinguishes between rites that mark the passage of an individual or social group from one status to another from those which mark transitions in the Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 37 passage of time (e.g. harvest, New Year), whereupon he goes on to explore ‘the basis of characteristic patterns in the order of ceremonies’ (1960: 10). Stressing the importance of transitions in any society, van Gennep then singles out rites of passage as a special category, consisting of three sub-categories, namely rites of separation, transition rites, and rites of incorporation. Van Gennep suggests calling the middle stage in a rite of passage a liminal period (ibid.: 11). He calls transition rites liminal rites, and the rites of incorporation postliminal rites. Van Gennep further explains that even though the tripartite structure is always recognizable and to some extent present, the rites of separation, transition and incorporation are not always equally important or equally elaborated in specific rituals. He also noted that the tripartite structure is sometimes reduplicated in the transitional period itself: in liminality proper, the sequence of separation, transition and incorporation is often present. The opening chapter is followed by a chapter on the spatial dimension of ritual passages. As we will return to below, van Gennep held the spatial dimension of thresholds, doors, gateways and other transit zones to be fundamental for the cultural elaboration of ritual transitions and cultural transformation. Chapter 3 is called Individuals and Groups. Besides opening up the society/individual question (a question dear to van Gennep, and one that made him an enemy of Durkheimian collectivism), the chapter is in fact a discussion of gift exchange as foundational for social bonds. The rest of the book follows an extremely simple structure, equivalent to a life cycle: pregnancy, birth, initiation, marriage, funeral. In other words, the book elegantly takes the narrative form of a life-cycle exactly as it describes the rituals by which such a life-cycle is made socially significant and meaningful within the variety of world cultures. Van Gennep’s later multi-volume work on French ethnology would be guided by a very similar structure, moving from conception and birth rites to death and funeral rites. 10 It is also for this reason that we can safely state that Rites of Passage was van Gennep’s most important work, and a ‘breakthrough’. It in many ways served to organize everything else he did. The book ends with a chapter that includes a variety of ‘other rituals’ that do not easily fit neatly into the life cycle structure (but nonetheless confirms the tripartite structure) before it concludes by stressing the universality of the whole argument. It would be wrong to accuse van Gennep of reductionism. By no means did he try to press all ritual forms into one explanatory framework. He simply noted an underlying pattern in rites that marked a passage from one state to another, without taking away or reducing all the other aspects or ‘individual purposes’ that such rites may also have (ibid.: 11). In other words – and this is important to stress – van Gennep’s work cannot be used directly to argue for any specific theory of rites. Van Gennep detected a pattern, a sequence, a ritual form. The ritual pattern was 10 Marcel Mauss, without here acknowledging Van Gennep’s quite evident influence, would later use the same approach in his attempt to delineate, not the structure of rites, but the very ‘nature of sociology’ (Mauss 1968: 9; see also Introduction to these works of Mauss by Mike Gane 2005). Liminality and the Modern 38 apparently universal: all societies use rites to demarcate transitions, and the forms taken by such rites of transition are comparable. And liminal spaces and moments are key to personal and social development, anywhere in the world. It is via the liminal that persons and groups are taken apart, recomposed and regenerated. Van Gennep was right. 1909–1914: Productive Years and First and Only Academic Position in Switzerland Let us take note: the book that made Arnold van Gennep famous half a century later, Rites of Passage, was written in a period when he was bursting with energy and dedication, amazingly prolific, and wholeheartedly engaged in contributing to the foundations of anthropology and sociology. And yet he could not pass that crucial threshold into French academia. He tried, again and again. In both 1907 and 1909 he applied for a position at Collège de France, but for reasons to be discussed below, he was never selected. From 1908 to 1912 he was technically speaking unemployed, living off his translations. Van Gennep was, all the while, building up a solid curriculum. Although he was still not an academic by profession, he became more and more engaged with his academic project. The final writing of Rites de Passage coincided with his decision to leave his job at the ministry and dedicate himself
